title: XXXII
html:
XXXII

From this moment I am to be considered as heir-apparent to the Shandy familyпїњвАФпїњвАФпїњand it is from this point properly, that the story of my Life and my Opinions sets out. With all my hurry and precipitation, I have but been clearing the ground to raise the buildingпїњвАФпїњвАФпїњand such a building do I foresee it will turn out, as never was planned, and as never was executed since Adam. In less than five minutes I shall have thrown my pen into the fire, and the little drop of thick ink which is left remaining at the bottom of my ink-horn, after itпїњвАФI have but half a score things to do in the timeпїњвАФпїњвАФпїњI have a thing to nameпїњвАФпїњвАФпїњa thing to lamentпїњвАФпїњвАФпїњa thing to hopeпїњвАФпїњвАФпїњa thing to promise, and a thing to threatenпїњвАФI have a thing to supposeпїњвАФa thing to declareпїњвАФпїњвАФпїњa thing to concealпїњвАФпїњвАФпїњa thing to choose, and a thing to pray forпїњвАФпїњвАФпїњвАФThis chapter, therefore, I name the chapter of ThingsпїњвАФпїњвАФпїњвАФand my next chapter to it, that is, the first chapter of my next volume, if I live, shall be my chapter upon whiskers, in order to keep up some sort of connection in my works.

The thing I lament is, that things have crowded in so thick upon me, that I have not been able to get into that part of my work, towards which I have all the way looked forwards, with so much earnest desire; and that is the Campaigns, but especially the amours of my uncle Toby, the events of which are of so singular a nature, and so Cervantick a cast, that if I can so manage it, as to convey but the same impressions to every other brain, which the occurrences themselves excite in my ownпїњвАФI will answer for it the book shall make its way in the world, much better than its master has done before it.пїњвАФпїњвАФпїњOh Tristram! Tristram! can this but be once brought aboutпїњвАФпїњвАФпїњthe credit, which will attend thee as an author, shall counterbalance the many evils which have befallen thee as a manпїњвАФпїњвАФпїњthou wilt feast upon the oneпїњвАФпїњвАФпїњwhen thou hast lost all sense and remembrance of the other!пїњвАФпїњвАФпїњ

No wonder I itch so much as I do, to get at these amoursпїњвАФThey are the choicest morsel of my whole story! and when I do get at вАЩemпїњвАФпїњвАФпїњassure yourselves, good folksпїњвАФ(nor do I value whose squeamish stomach takes offence at it) I shall not be at all nice in the choice of my words!пїњвАФпїњвАФпїњand thatвАЩs the thing I have to declare.пїњвАФпїњвАФпїњвАФI shall never get all through in five minutes, that I fearпїњвАФпїњвАФпїњand the thing I hope is, that your worships and reverences are not offendedпїњвАФif you are, depend uponвАЩt IвАЩll give you something, my good gentry, next year to be offended atпїњвАФпїњвАФпїњthatвАЩs my dear JennyвАЩs wayпїњвАФbut who my Jenny isпїњвАФand which is the right and which the wrong end of a woman, is the thing to be concealedпїњвАФit shall be told you in the next chapter but one to my chapter of ButtonholesпїњвАФпїњвАФпїњand not one chapter before.

And now that you have just got to the end of these four volumesпїњвАФпїњвАФпїњthe thing I have to ask is, how you feel your heads? my own akes dismally!пїњвАФпїњвАФпїњвАФas for your healths, I know, they are much better.пїњвАФTrue Shandeism, think what you will against it, opens the heart and lungs, and like all those affections which partake of its nature, it forces the blood and other vital fluids of the body to run freely through its channels, makes the wheel of life run long and cheerfully round.

Was I left, like Sancho Panca, to choose my kingdom, it should not be maritimeпїњвАФor a kingdom of blacks to make a penny of;пїњвАФno, it should be a kingdom of hearty laughing subjects: And as the bilious and more saturnine passions, by creating disorders in the blood and humours, have as bad an influence, I see, upon the body politick as body naturalпїњвАФпїњвАФпїњand as nothing but a habit of virtue can fully govern those passions, and subject them to reasonпїњвАФпїњвАФпїњвАФI should add to my prayerпїњвАФthat God would give my subjects grace to be as wise as they were merry; and then should I be the happiest monarch, and they the happiest people under heaven.

And so, with this moral for the present, may it please your worships and your reverences, I take my leave of you till this time twelvemonth, when, (unless this vile cough kills me in the meantime) IвАЩll have another pluck at your beards, and lay open a story to the world you little dream of.
